- **MATLAB essentials for safety computing:** `wblfit` (Weibull MLE), `exprnd` (exponential random), `integral` (numerical integration), `binofit` (binomial parameter estimation), `chi2inv` (confidence intervals for Poisson failure data).
- **Monte Carlo convergence** requires **$N \ge 100{,}000$** iterations for rare-event probabilities ($P < 10^{-4}$).
- **Weibull MTTF**: $\text{MTTF} = \eta \cdot \Gamma(1/\beta + 1)$. Memorize the **Gamma function** values: $\Gamma(1) = 1$, $\Gamma(2) = 1$, $\Gamma(1.5) = \sqrt{\pi}/2 \approx 0.886$.
- **Independence assumption** is non-negotiable when applying series/parallel formulas. If components share a common cause failure, a **beta-factor model** must be used instead.
- **MATLAB vectorized operations** (e.g., `prod`, `cumprod`) are preferred over explicit `for` loops for reliability calculations, both for **performance** and **readability**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Application of System Simulation in Solving Plant Safety Problems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCSFL608 - RELIABILITY AND COMPUTATIONAL LABORATORY
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Application of System Simulation in Solving Plant Safety Problems

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **System Simulation in Plant Safety** is a computational methodology that employs mathematical models, numerical methods, and software tools to replicate the physical, chemical, and operational behavior of an industrial plant under normal, abnormal, and emergency conditions, in order to predict, evaluate, and mitigate potential hazards to personnel, equipment, and the environment.

In the context of the **KTU 2024 Scheme (PCSFL608)**, system simulation is treated as a *virtual experimentation* technique where real plant scenarios—such as gas leaks, runaway reactions, fire, explosion, and toxic dispersion—are modeled mathematically and analyzed through digital computation before being executed in the actual plant.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The Flight Simulator**
>
> Imagine a pilot learning to fly a Boeing 747. The pilot does **not** learn by crashing real aircraft. Instead, a **flight simulator** reproduces every system (engines, hydraulics, hydraulics, weather, terrain) inside a computer. The pilot can push buttons, fail systems, face emergencies, and observe outcomes—all safely.
>
> Similarly, in a chemical plant:
> - The **"aircraft"** = the real plant (refinery, LPG terminal, ammonia storage).
> - The **"simulator"** = a software model built using thermodynamics, fluid dynamics, kinetics, and reliability data.
> - The **"emergency scenarios"** = pump failure, valve leakage, overpressure, fire, explosion, toxic release.
>
> This allows safety engineers to ask **"What if?"** questions without endangering lives.

### 1.3 Classification of System Simulation in Plant Safety

| Category | Description | Example |
|----------|-------------|---------|
| **Steady-State Simulation** | Solves algebraic equations for a fixed operating point | Distillation column design |
| **Dynamic Simulation** | Solves differential equations over time | Compressor surge, depressurization |
| **Monte Carlo Simulation** | Uses random sampling to estimate probabilistic outcomes | Risk assessment, reliability |
| **Discrete Event Simulation (DES)** | Models events occurring at discrete time intervals | Shutdown system logic |
| **Continuous Simulation** | Models state variables changing continuously | Pressure vessel venting |
| **3D Computational Fluid Dynamics (CFD)** | Solves Navier-Stokes equations numerically | Gas cloud dispersion |

### 1.4 Standard Metrics Used

The following standard metrics are universally applied in KTU-aligned safety computations:

- **Failure Rate (λ)** — failures per million hours, typically in **failures/10⁶ hr**.
- **Mean Time Between Failures (MTBF)** — typically expressed in **hours**.
- **Probability of Failure on Demand (PFD)** — dimensionless, range [0, 1].
- **Individual Risk (IR)** — expressed as **fatalities per year (fatalities/yr)**.
- **SIL (Safety Integrity Level)** — IEC 61508 levels **SIL 1, SIL 2, SIL 3, SIL 4**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Concept — Phases of Plant Safety Simulation

The simulation exercise proceeds through five sequential logic steps. Each step has a clear **"why"** and **"how"**.

1. **Step 1 — Problem Definition**
   - *Why:* Without a clear hazard definition, simulation produces irrelevant data.
   - *How:* Identify the unwanted event (e.g., LPG leak from a bullet tank).

2. **Step 2 — Conceptual Modeling**
   - *Why:* Defines the boundary of the system.
   - *How:* Sketch process flow, identify equipment, isolation, ESD valves.

3. **Step 3 — Mathematical Modeling**
   - *Why:* Translates the physical system into solvable equations.
   - *How:* Apply mass, energy, momentum balances; Arrhenius kinetics; equation of state.

4. **Step 4 — Numerical Solution & Computation**
   - *Why:* Most safety equations are non-linear ODEs/PDEs, no closed-form solution.
   - *How:* Use Euler, Runge-Kutta, or finite volume methods.

5. **Step 5 — Validation, Interpretation & Risk Quantification**
   - *Why:* A simulation is only as good as its validation.
   - *How:* Compare against plant data, layer of protection analysis (LOPA), and QRA.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following formulas are repeatedly tested in KTU ESE for **PCS608 / PCSFL608** under the Reliability & Safety domain.

| # | Formula | Description | Units / Range |
|---|---------|-------------|----------------|
| 1 | $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$ | Reliability function (exponential distribution) | dimensionless |
| 2 | $F(t) = 1 - e^{-\lambda t}$ | Cumulative failure probability (unreliability) | dimensionless |
| 3 | $f(t) = \lambda e^{-\lambda t}$ | Probability density function (PDF) of failure | 1/hr |
| 4 | $MTBF = \dfrac{1}{\lambda}$ | Mean Time Between Failures | hours |
| 5 | $PFD_{avg} = \dfrac{\lambda_{DU} \cdot T_{proof}}{2}$ | Probability of Failure on Demand (IEC 61508) | dimensionless |
| 6 | $Risk = Frequency \times Consequence$ | Classical risk equation | fatality/yr |
| 7 | $P_{OR}(t) = 1 - \prod_{i=1}^{n}(1 - P_{i}(t))$ | OR-gate probability in fault tree | dimensionless |
| 8 | $P_{AND}(t) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} P_{i}(t)$ | AND-gate probability in fault tree | dimensionless |
| 9 | $\dfrac{dC}{dt} = -u \cdot \nabla C + D \cdot \nabla^{2} C$ | Advection-diffusion equation (toxic dispersion) | mol/(m³·s) |
| 10 | $Q = C_{d} \cdot A \cdot \sqrt{2 \cdot \dfrac{\Delta P}{\rho}}$ | Compressible gas leak rate (orifice model) | kg/s |
| 11 | $t_{detection} = -\dfrac{\ln(1 - 0.95)}{\lambda_{sensor}}$ | Sensor detection time at 95% confidence | seconds |
| 12 | $N_{fatalities} = \int_{0}^{\infty} P(C) \cdot f_{ignition} \cdot C \, dC$ | Expected fatality integral (QRA) | fatalities |

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

System simulation is applied across the entire hydrocarbon value chain:

- **Oil & Gas Refineries** — Distillation column relief valve sizing.
- **LNG Terminals** — Boiling Liquid Expanding Vapor Explosion (BLEVE) modeling.
- **Ammonia Plants** — Toxic ammonia dispersion (AEGL, ERPG limits).
- **Pharmaceutical** — Reactor runaway simulation using ARC (Accelerating Rate Calorimetry) data.
- **Power Plants** — Boiler tube rupture simulation, turbine overspeed trip reliability.

The **utility** of simulation is to **reduce uncertainty**, **comply with regulatory standards** (OSHA, EPA, OISD, PESO), and **optimize safety instrumented functions (SIF)**.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of Reliability Function from Failure Rate

We begin from the fundamental definition of failure rate for a non-repairable component.

> [!NOTE]
> **Objective:** Derive the closed-form reliability expression $R(t)$ from the hazard function $h(t)$.

**Step 1 — Definition of Hazard Function**

The instantaneous failure rate $h(t)$ (also called the hazard rate) is defined as the conditional probability that a component fails in the next infinitesimal interval $\Delta t$, *given* it has survived up to time $t$.

$$
h(t) = \lim_{\Delta t \to 0} \dfrac{P(t \le T \le t + \Delta t \mid T > t)}{\Delta t}
$$

**Step 2 — Express as Probability Density**

By definition of conditional probability:

$$
P(t \le T \le t + \Delta t \mid T > t) = \dfrac{f(t) \cdot \Delta t}{R(t)}
$$

Substituting back:

$$
h(t) = \dfrac{f(t)}{R(t)}
$$

**Step 3 — Substituting the PDF-Relationship**

We know that the cumulative distribution function (CDF) is $F(t) = 1 - R(t)$. Differentiating:

$$
f(t) = \dfrac{dF(t)}{dt} = -\dfrac{dR(t)}{dt}
$$

Plug this into the hazard equation:

$$
h(t) = \dfrac{-dR(t)/dt}{R(t)} = -\dfrac{1}{R(t)} \cdot \dfrac{dR(t)}{dt}
$$

**Step 4 — Separate Variables**

$$
h(t)\, dt = -\dfrac{dR(t)}{R(t)}
$$

**Step 5 — Integrate Both Sides from 0 to t**

Assuming initial reliability $R(0) = 1$ (component starts in working state):

$$
\int_{0}^{t} h(\tau)\, d\tau = -\int_{1}^{R(t)} \dfrac{dR}{R} = -\ln R(t)
$$

**Step 6 — Solve for R(t)**

$$
\ln R(t) = -\int_{0}^{t} h(\tau)\, d\tau
$$

$$
R(t) = \exp\left[-\int_{0}^{t} h(\tau)\, d\tau\right]
$$

**Step 7 — Apply the Constant Failure Rate Assumption**

For most industrial components in their *useful life phase*, the failure rate is approximately constant: $h(t) = \lambda$. This yields the famous *exponential reliability formula*:

$$
R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}
$$

This derivation is foundational because the exponential distribution is the **backbone of all reliability block diagrams, fault trees, and Markov models** used in plant safety simulation.

### 3.2 Derivation of the PFD (Probability of Failure on Demand)

> [!NOTE]
> **Objective:** Derive the average Probability of Failure on Demand for a single-channel safety instrumented function (SIF) per IEC 61508.

**Step 1 — Define the System**

A simple SIF consists of three subsystems in series: a sensor, a logic solver (PLC), and a final element (valve). Each has a *dangerous undetected* failure rate $\lambda_{DU}$.

**Step 2 — Probability of Surviving Up to Time t**

From the exponential model:

$$
R(t) = e^{-\lambda_{DU} t}
$$

**Step 3 — Expected Unavailability Over One Proof-Test Interval**

The average unavailability $\overline{PFD}$ over an interval $[0, T_{proof}]$ is obtained by integrating the unreliability and dividing by the interval length.

$$
\overline{PFD} = \dfrac{1}{T_{proof}} \int_{0}^{T_{proof}} \left(1 - e^{-\lambda_{DU} t}\right) dt
$$

**Step 4 — Expand the Integral**

$$
\overline{PFD} = \dfrac{1}{T_{proof}} \left[\int_{0}^{T_{proof}} 1 \, dt - \int_{0}^{T_{proof}} e^{-\lambda_{DU} t} \, dt\right]
$$

**Step 5 — Evaluate the Second Integral**

$$
\int_{0}^{T_{proof}} e^{-\lambda_{DU} t} \, dt = \dfrac{1 - e^{-\lambda_{DU} T_{proof}}}{\lambda_{DU}}
$$

**Step 6 — Substitute Back**

$$
\overline{PFD} = \dfrac{1}{T_{proof}} \left[T_{proof} - \dfrac{1 - e^{-\lambda_{DU} T_{proof}}}{\lambda_{DU}}\right]
$$

**Step 7 — Apply the Small-Failure-Rate Approximation**

For typical industrial values ($\lambda_{DU} \cdot T_{proof} \ll 1$), the Taylor expansion $e^{-x} \approx 1 - x + \dfrac{x^2}{2}$ gives:

$$
1 - e^{-\lambda_{DU} T_{proof}} \approx \lambda_{DU} T_{proof} - \dfrac{(\lambda_{DU} T_{proof})^{2}}{2}
$$

Substituting and simplifying:

$$
\overline{PFD} \approx \dfrac{1}{T_{proof}} \cdot \dfrac{(\lambda_{DU} T_{proof})^{2}}{2 \lambda_{DU}}
$$

$$
\boxed{\overline{PFD} \approx \dfrac{\lambda_{DU} \cdot T_{proof}}{2}}
$$

This result directly links component reliability data to the **SIL classification** required for plant safety compliance.

### 3.3 Monte Carlo Simulation of Plant Risk (Illustrative)

For a complex plant with *N* independent failure modes, the true risk is computed by drawing random samples from each component's failure distribution and aggregating the outcomes.

**Step 1 — Generate Random Failure Times**

For each component $i$, sample a random time to failure from the exponential distribution:

$$
T_{i} = -\dfrac{\ln(U_{i})}{\lambda_{i}}, \quad U_{i} \sim \text{Uniform}(0,1)
$$

**Step 2 — Determine System Status**

The system fails at time $T_{sys} = \min(T_1, T_2, \ldots, T_N)$ for a parallel configuration, or $T_{sys} = \max(T_1, T_2, \ldots, T_N)$ for a series configuration.

**Step 3 — Repeat M Times**

After $M$ iterations, the system reliability is estimated as:

$$
\hat{R}(t) = \dfrac{\text{Number of trials where } T_{sys} > t}{M}
$$

**Step 4 — Convergence**

By the *Law of Large Numbers*, the estimate converges to the true reliability as $M \to \infty$. Typical KTU computations require $M \geq 10^{5}$ for 95% confidence.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of Plant Safety Simulation

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Real Plant Hazard Scenario] --> B[Hazard Identification HAZID]
    B --> C[Conceptual Process Model]
    C --> D[Mathematical Model Formulation]
    D --> E[Numerical Solver ODE / PDE / Monte Carlo]
    E --> F[Result Database Time-Series of T, P, C]
    F --> G[Consequence Analysis Thermal Radiation Overpressure Toxic]
    G --> H[Risk Quantification Frequency vs Consequence]
    H --> I[Decision Module Mitigation / SIL / EHS Compliance]
    I --> J[Safety Report and Recommendation]
    D --> K[Reliability Database Failure Rates MTBF]
    K --> E
    F --> L[Validation Against Plant Data]
    L --> C
```

### 4.2 Fault Tree Simulation Flow for an LPG Bullet Tank

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Top Event LPG Bullet Catastrophic Rupture] --> B[Internal Overpressure]
    A --> C[External Impact]
    B --> D[Relief Valve Fails to Open]
    B --> E[Excessive Heat Input Fire Exposure]
    D --> F[OR Gate Mechanical Jam]
    D --> G[OR Gate Pilot Failure]
    F --> H[OR Gate Spring Failure]
    F --> I[OR Guide Corrosion]
    G --> J[OR Sensor Failure]
    G --> K[OR Signal Line Blockage]
    E --> L[AND Gate Pool Fire Present]
    E --> M[AND No Water Spray Activation]
    C --> N[AND Vehicle Collision]
    C --> O[AND Bund Wall Failure]
```

### 4.3 Monte Carlo Simulation Loop

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Initialize M Iterations] --> B[Draw Random Numbers Uniform 0 1]
    B --> C[Compute T_i from Exponential PDF]
    C --> D[Apply System Logic Series or Parallel]
    D --> E[Record T_system and Outcome]
    E --> F{Achieved M iterations?}
    F -->|No| B
    F -->|Yes| G[Compute Reliability R_hat t]
    G --> H[Compute Risk Frequency times Consequence]
    H --> I[Generate Histogram and Confidence Interval]
```

### 4.4 Layer of Protection Analysis LOPA Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Initiating Cause Pump Deadhead] --> B[Process Design Base Layer]
    B --> C[Instrumented Layer BPCS Alarm Trip]
    C --> D[Safety Instrumented Layer SIF SIL]
    D --> E[Mechanical Layer Relief Valve Rupture Disc]
    E --> F[Procedural Layer Operator Response]
    F --> G[Mitigated Event Frequency Achieved]
    G --> H{Risk Tolerable?}
    H -->|No| I[Add Independent Protection Layer IPL]
    I --> D
    H -->|Yes| J[Safety Case Approved]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper Set B]**

> Define *System Simulation* as applied to plant safety problems. List any **two** industrial scenarios where dynamic simulation is essential.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

System simulation in plant safety is the computational representation of a real industrial process using mathematical models (mass, energy, momentum balances, kinetics, and reliability data) executed on a digital computer to predict the response of the plant under normal, abnormal, and emergency conditions.

Two scenarios requiring dynamic simulation are:

1. **Depressurization of a hydrocarbon vessel** — to size the Emergency Depressurization System (EDP) per API 521.
2. **Runaway reaction in a batch reactor** — to determine the time-to-maximum-rate (TMR) and relief vent area per DIERS methodology.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Supple Model]**

> State the **exponential reliability formula** and define each term. Why is this distribution preferred for industrial safety simulations?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The exponential reliability formula is:

$$
R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}
$$

where $R(t)$ is the reliability at time $t$ (dimensionless), $e$ is Euler's number, $\lambda$ is the constant failure rate (per hour), and $t$ is the operating time (hours).

This distribution is preferred because most industrial components (valves, pumps, sensors) operate in the **useful-life phase** of the *bathtub curve*, where the failure rate is approximately constant. It yields a *memoryless* property that simplifies Markov chain modeling of Safety Instrumented Functions (SIF).

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

> **A process vessel containing LPG is protected by a pressure safety valve (PSV) and a bursting disc in series. The vessel is located in a refinery where the ambient temperature can rise to 60 °C. Using system simulation, evaluate the failure probability of the combined protection system.**
>
> **(a)** Develop a fault tree for the top event *“Loss of Containment of LPG”*. Calculate the probability of failure for the top event, given the following component failure data:
>
> | Component | Failure Rate (λ) per 10⁶ hr |
> |-----------|------------------------------|
> | Pressure Sensor | 2.5 |
> | Logic Solver (PLC) | 0.8 |
> | PSV (fails to open) | 1.5 |
> | Bursting Disc | 0.4 |
> | Manual Isolation Valve (closed by mistake) | 5.0 |
>
> Mission time $t$ = 8760 hours (1 year). Assume exponential distribution and that the burst disc and PSV are in **parallel** redundancy, while the isolation valve acts in **series** with the parallel combination.
>
> **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** Suggest **two** engineering improvements that could reduce the SIL risk and recompute the top-event probability if a **2-out-of-3 voting** logic is applied to the PSVs. **(7 marks)**

---

### **Model Solution for Question A**

#### (a) Fault Tree Construction & Probability Calculation

**Step 1 — Component Unreliabilities**

Using $F(t) = 1 - e^{-\lambda t}$ with $\lambda$ in per hour ($\lambda = \text{value} \times 10^{-6}$ per hr):

For mission time $t = 8760$ hr:

- Pressure Sensor: $F_{PS} = 1 - e^{-2.5 \times 10^{-6} \times 8760} = 1 - e^{-0.0219} = 1 - 0.9783 = 0.0217$
- Logic Solver: $F_{LS} = 1 - e^{-0.8 \times 10^{-6} \times 8760} = 1 - e^{-0.0070} = 0.0070$
- PSV: $F_{PSV} = 1 - e^{-1.5 \times 10^{-6} \times 8760} = 1 - e^{-0.0131} = 0.0130$
- Bursting Disc: $F_{BD} = 1 - e^{-0.4 \times 10^{-6} \times 8760} = 1 - e^{-0.0035} = 0.0035$
- Isolation Valve: $F_{IV} = 1 - e^{-5.0 \times 10^{-6} \times 8760} = 1 - e^{-0.0438} = 0.0428$

**Step 2 — PSV and Bursting Disc are in Parallel (OR Gate for Failure)**

For an OR gate, the system fails only if **all** components fail. Since the parallel reliability formula is $R_{parallel} = 1 - \prod_{i}(1 - R_i) = 1 - \prod_i F_i$, we compute unreliability:

$$
F_{parallel} = F_{PSV} \times F_{BD} = 0.0130 \times 0.0035 = 4.55 \times 10^{-5}
$$

**Step 3 — Pressure Sensor, Logic Solver, and the Parallel Combination are in Series (AND Gate for Failure)**

For an AND gate, the system fails if **any** component fails (the system is robust only if all are functional):

$$
F_{system} = 1 - (1 - F_{PS})(1 - F_{LS})(1 - F_{parallel})
$$

Calculate the survivor terms:

- $(1 - F_{PS}) = 0.9783$
- $(1 - F_{LS}) = 0.9930$
- $(1 - F_{parallel}) = 1 - 4.55 \times 10^{-5} = 0.99995$

$$
F_{system} = 1 - (0.9783 \times 0.9930 \times 0.99995)
$$

$$
F_{system} = 1 - 0.9713 = 0.0287
$$

**Step 4 — Include the Isolation Valve in Series**

The isolation valve is also in series, so it multiplies into the failure probability of the unprotected branches:

For simplicity, since the isolation valve acts as an additional series failure mode, the overall top-event unreliability becomes:

$$
F_{top} \approx 1 - (1 - F_{system})(1 - F_{IV})
$$

$$
F_{top} = 1 - (1 - 0.0287)(1 - 0.0428)
$$

$$
F_{top} = 1 - (0.9713 \times 0.9572) = 1 - 0.9297 = 0.0703
$$

**Result:** The probability of loss of containment over one year is approximately **0.0703**, i.e., **7.03%**.

#### (b) Engineering Improvements & 2oo3 Voting

**Improvement 1 — Add a 2-out-of-3 (2oo3) Voting PSV System**

In 2oo3 logic, the system requires at least two of three PSVs to open successfully. The probability that at least two out of three PSVs fail is calculated using the binomial distribution:

$$
P(k \geq 2 \text{ failures out of 3}) = \binom{3}{2} F_{PSV}^{2}(1-F_{PSV}) + \binom{3}{3} F_{PSV}^{3}
$$

$$
P = 3 \times (0.0130)^2 \times 0.9870 + (0.0130)^3
$$

$$
P = 3 \times 1.69 \times 10^{-4} \times 0.987 + 2.197 \times 10^{-6}
$$

$$
P = 5.00 \times 10^{-4} + 2.20 \times 10^{-6} \approx 5.02 \times 10^{-4}
$$

This is significantly lower than the single PSV unreliability of $0.0130$.

**Improvement 2 — Install a Continuous Online Diagnostic on the Logic Solver**

Adding online diagnostics reduces the dangerous undetected failure rate $\lambda_{DU}$ by a factor of 50–90% (IEC 61508). If $\lambda_{LS}$ is reduced from $0.8 \times 10^{-6}$ to $0.1 \times 10^{-6}$ per hr:

$$
F_{LS}^{new} = 1 - e^{-0.1 \times 10^{-6} \times 8760} = 1 - e^{-0.000876} \approx 8.76 \times 10^{-4}
$$

The new top-event probability drops by an order of magnitude, pushing the system toward **SIL 2/SIL 3** compliance.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam — June 2024]

> **(a)** With the help of a **block diagram**, describe the major steps involved in performing a **dynamic simulation** for depressurization of a hydrocarbon vessel during a fire scenario. Mention the governing differential equations. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** A horizontal cylindrical vessel of diameter **D = 2 m** and length **L = 6 m** contains propane at **15 °C, 8 bar**. The fire engulfment heat input is **Q = 110 kW/m²**. The set pressure of the PSV is **10 bar (g)**. Using API 521 guidelines, compute the **required relief area** if the discharge coefficient $C_d = 0.9$ and the specific heat ratio $k = 1.13$. **(7 marks)**

---

### **Model Solution for Question B**

#### (a) Major Steps in Dynamic Simulation of Fire Depressurization

**Step 1 — Define Wetted Surface Area**

Wetted area exposed to fire:

$$
A_{wetted} = \pi D L + 2 \cdot \dfrac{\pi D^2}{4}
$$

**Step 2 — Set Up Energy Balance**

The vessel wall heat input is governed by:

$$
\dot{Q}_{in} = Q \cdot A_{wetted} \cdot F_{env}
$$

where $F_{env}$ is the environmental factor (1.0 for total engulfment).

**Step 3 — Mass and Energy Balances (Governing ODEs)**

$$
\dfrac{d(m \cdot u)}{dt} = \dot{Q}_{in} - \dot{m}_{out} \cdot h_{out}
$$

$$
\dfrac{dm}{dt} = -\dot{m}_{out}
$$

where $u$ is internal energy, $h$ is enthalpy, and $\dot{m}_{out}$ is the relief mass flow rate.

**Step 4 — Discharge Flow Model**

For compressible flow through the relief valve:

$$
\dot{m}_{out} = C_d \cdot A \cdot P \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{k \cdot M}{R \cdot T} \left(\dfrac{2}{k+1}\right)^{\frac{k+1}{k-1}}}
$$

(critical/subsonic nozzle equation).

**Step 5 — Solve Numerically**

Use Runge-Kutta 4th order or stiff ODE solvers (e.g., LSODE) with a small time step ($\Delta t \le 1$ s) to track pressure and temperature evolution.

**Step 6 — Validate**

Compare simulated peak pressure with the PSV set pressure; if $P_{peak} > P_{set}$, increase $A$ (relief area) and re-run.

#### (b) Relief Area Computation

**Step 1 — Wetted Area**

$$
A_{wetted} = \pi (2)(6) + 2 \cdot \dfrac{\pi (2)^2}{4} = 37.7 + 6.28 = 43.98 \, m^2
$$

**Step 2 — Heat Input**

$$
\dot{Q}_{in} = 110 \times 10^{3} \times 43.98 \times 1.0 = 4.838 \times 10^{6} \, W = 4838 \, kW
$$

**Step 3 — Convert Set Pressure to Absolute**

$$
P_{set} = 10 + 1.013 = 11.013 \, \text{bar} = 1.1013 \times 10^{6} \, \text{Pa}
$$

**Step 4 — Compressible Flow Through Orifice (API 521 Equation)**

For critical flow of propane:

$$
\dot{m} = C_d \cdot A \cdot P_{set} \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{k \cdot M}{R \cdot T} \cdot \left(\dfrac{2}{k+1}\right)^{\frac{k+1}{k-1}}}
$$

Compute the bracketed term:

- $k = 1.13$
- $M = 44.097$ g/mol = 0.0441 kg/mol
- $R = 8.314$ J/(mol·K)
- $T = 15 + 273 = 288$ K

$$
\left(\dfrac{2}{k+1}\right)^{\frac{k+1}{k-1}} = \left(\dfrac{2}{2.13}\right)^{\frac{2.13}{0.13}} = (0.939)^{16.38} \approx 0.359
$$

$$
\sqrt{\dfrac{1.13 \times 0.0441}{8.314 \times 288} \times 0.359} = \sqrt{\dfrac{0.0498}{2394.4} \times 0.359} = \sqrt{7.47 \times 10^{-6}} = 2.733 \times 10^{-3}
$$

**Step 5 — Required Mass Flow Rate**

Using the API 521 simplified formula:

$$
\dot{m}_{req} = \dfrac{\dot{Q}_{in}}{\Delta h_{vap}} = \dfrac{4838 \, kW}{425 \, kJ/kg} = 11.38 \, kg/s
$$

(where $\Delta h_{vap}$ of propane ≈ 425 kJ/kg).

**Step 6 — Solve for Relief Area**

$$
A = \dfrac{\dot{m}_{req}}{C_d \cdot P_{set} \cdot 2.733 \times 10^{-3}}
$$

$$
A = \dfrac{11.38}{0.9 \times 1.1013 \times 10^{6} \times 2.733 \times 10^{-3}}
$$

$$
A = \dfrac{11.38}{2708.4} = 4.20 \times 10^{-3} \, m^{2} = 42.0 \, cm^{2}
$$

**Result:** The required relief area is approximately **42 cm²**, corresponding to a 2-inch or larger PSV inlet.

---

### ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Places Where KTU Students Lose Marks in Plant Safety Simulation Problems:**
>
> 1. **Unit Mismatch** — Mixing bar with Pa, kW with W, or failure rate units ($10^{-6}$/hr vs 1/hr). Always declare units.
> 2. **Forgot to Convert Gauge to Absolute Pressure** — A recurring 1-mark loss in relief-area problems.
> 3. **Confusing OR vs AND Gates** — In fault trees, *OR* means failure of **any** component causes system failure; *AND* means *all* must fail.
> 4. **Skipping the Taylor Approximation Justification** — When deriving $PFD_{avg} = \lambda T_{proof}/2$, KTU examiners award a mark for stating the small-rate assumption.
> 5. **No Conclusion Statement** — Always end with **"Therefore, the required relief area is … cm²"** or **"Therefore, the SIF meets SIL 2"** for full marks.
> 6. **Wrong Vapour Enthalpy for Propane** — Use 425 kJ/kg (not 425 J/kg) — a common 1-mark trap.

---

### 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ System simulation replaces risky real-world experimentation with virtual hazard analysis.
- ✅ The **exponential reliability formula** $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$ is the *single most tested equation* in KTU safety modules.
- ✅ Fault trees use **OR gates for failure aggregation in parallel redundancy** and **AND gates for series configuration**.
- ✅ The **PFD average** for proof-tested systems is $\overline{PFD} = \lambda_{DU} \cdot T_{proof}/2$, foundational for IEC 61508 SIL.
- ✅ API 521 and DIERS are the two *primary references* for relief sizing in fire scenarios.
- ✅ Monte Carlo simulation requires at least **10⁵ iterations** for statistical convergence.
- ✅ CFD-based dispersion (advection-diffusion) requires solving the Navier-Stokes equations coupled with species transport.
- ✅ Always quote **units** in the final answer to avoid losing the 1-mark "units" award.
- ✅ Remember the **environmental factor** $F_{env} = 1.0$ for total fire engulfment in API 521.
- ✅ The three pillars of plant safety simulation are: **Hazard Identification → Consequence Modeling → Risk Quantification**.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Fire and Smoke Modelling Using Open-Source Software
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCSFL608 - RELIABILITY AND COMPUTATIONAL LABORATORY
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Fire and Smoke Modelling Using Open-Source Software

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Fire and Smoke Modelling** is the application of Computational Fluid Dynamics (CFD) and thermophysical principles to numerically simulate the initiation, growth, spread, and decay of a fire, along with the transport of combustion products (smoke, toxic gases, soot) within a built environment or open atmosphere. The simulation predicts spatially and temporally resolved fields of temperature, velocity, visibility, and species concentration.

In the **KTU 2024 Scheme** context, this experiment introduces students to **zone models** (two-zone, single-zone) and **field models** (CFD-based) implemented in open-source platforms, primarily the **Fire Dynamics Simulator (FDS)** developed by the National Institute of Standards and Technology (NIST, USA) and visualised through its companion tool **Smokeview (SMV)**.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Weather Forecast" for a Building Fire

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy:** Imagine you pour ink into a fish tank. You cannot predict exactly where the ink will be in 5 seconds without knowing the water currents, tank shape, and gravity. Now scale that up: smoke from a fire is the "ink," and the building is the "fish tank." **Fire and smoke modelling solves the equations of fluid motion to forecast where the smoke will travel, how hot it will get, and how long occupants have to escape.**

The software does not "draw" the fire — it solves the **conservation equations** of mass, momentum, and energy for millions of tiny cells in 3D space, second by second, to produce a digital twin of the fire event.

### 1.3 Open-Source Tools Commonly Used in KTU Labs

| Tool | Type | Primary Role |
|------|------|--------------|
| **FDS (Fire Dynamics Simulator)** | Field (CFD) Solver | Core numerical engine — solves Navier–Stokes + combustion + radiation |
| **Smokeview (SMV)** | Post-Processor | 3D visualisation of FDS output (isosurfaces, vectors, slices) |
| **CFAST (Consolidated Fire and Smoke Transport)** | Zone Model | Two-zone smoke layer / ceiling jet estimation |
| **OpenFOAM (fireFoam solver)** | Field (CFD) Solver | Research-grade LES / RANS fire simulations |
| **Blender (Mantaflow)** | Visual Smoke Simulator | Quick visual smoke rendering (non-physical) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** At KTU, the laboratory typically uses **FDS + Smokeview** because they are: (i) completely free, (ii) officially maintained by NIST, (iii) documented with peer-reviewed verification (Sweeny & Gutmacher, 2014 NIST Special Publication), and (iv) accepted by **NFPA, SFPE, and IBC** for performance-based fire design submissions.

### 1.4 Why Perform Fire and Smoke Modelling?

- **Life Safety:** Predict **Available Safe Egress Time (ASET)** vs. **Required Safe Egress Time (RSET)**.
- **Code Compliance:** Demonstrate equivalence to prescriptive **National Building Code (NBC 2016, Part 4)** requirements.
- **Smoke Control System Design:** Size **mechanical extract fans, stairwell pressurisation, smoke curtains**.
- **Detector & Sprinkler Activation:** Forecast **detection time** and **sprinkler link operation**.
- **Forensic Investigation:** Reconstruct the fire timeline when physical evidence is destroyed.
- **Structural Fire Engineering:** Compute **thermal exposure** on steel/concrete members.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Performance-Based Design (PBD)** in the KTU syllabus explicitly uses fire modelling outputs to demonstrate that occupants can evacuate safely — replacing rigid prescriptive rules with engineered evidence.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Physics Behind the Simulation

Fire modelling is the simultaneous numerical solution of the following coupled conservation equations, discretised on a 3D Cartesian mesh:

**A. Mass Continuity (Incompressible-Low-Mach Formulation)**

$$
\frac{\partial \rho}{\partial t} + \nabla \cdot (\rho \mathbf{u}) = 0
$$

**B. Momentum (Filtered Navier–Stokes for LES)**

$$
\frac{\partial (\rho \mathbf{u})}{\partial t} + \nabla \cdot (\rho \mathbf{u}\mathbf{u}) = -\nabla p + \rho \mathbf{g} + \nabla \cdot \boldsymbol{\tau} + \mathbf{f}_b
$$

where the viscous stress tensor is

$$
\boldsymbol{\tau} = \mu \left[ \nabla \mathbf{u} + (\nabla \mathbf{u})^T - \tfrac{2}{3} (\nabla \cdot \mathbf{u}) \mathbf{I} \right]
$$

**C. Energy (Enthalpy Transport)**

$$
\frac{\partial (\rho h)}{\partial t} + \nabla \cdot (\rho h \mathbf{u}) = \frac{Dp}{Dt} + \dot{q}''' - \nabla \cdot \dot{\mathbf{q}}''_r
$$

**D. Species Transport (Combustion Products)**

$$
\frac{\partial (\rho Y_\alpha)}{\partial t} + \nabla \cdot (\rho Y_\alpha \mathbf{u}) = \nabla \cdot (\rho D_\alpha \nabla Y_\alpha) + \dot{m}'''_\alpha
$$

**E. Equation of State (Ideal Gas, Low-Mach Approximation)**

$$
p_0(t) = \rho R T \sum_\alpha \frac{Y_\alpha}{W_\alpha}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why "Low-Mach"?** Fires produce temperature changes of hundreds of degrees, but pressure waves travel at the speed of sound. By separating the thermodynamic pressure $p_0(t)$ from the hydrodynamic pressure $p$, FDS solves the equations *incompressibly* — a 100× speed gain with negligible loss of accuracy for fire-driven flows.

### 2.2 Sub-Models Required to Close the System

- **Turbulence Model:** FDS defaults to **Large Eddy Simulation (LES)** with the Deardorff sub-grid model. RANS ($k$–$\varepsilon$) is available but rarely used for accuracy.
- **Combustion Model:** Single-step, infinite-rate (mixing-controlled) reaction with a default stoichiometry: $\mathrm{C_{3.6}H_6.2O_{2.4} + 5.3(O_2 + 3.76 N_2) \rightarrow 3.6 CO_2 + 3.1 H_2O + 19.9 N_2}$. Mixture fraction $Z$ is tracked.
- **Radiation Model:** Finite Volume Method (FVM) solver of the **Radiative Transfer Equation (RTE)** with a grey-gas assumption. The radiation absorption coefficient is computed using a narrow-band model $\kappa = \kappa_1 T + \kappa_2$.
- **Pyrolysis Model:** Solid-phase 1D heat conduction, Arrhenius kinetics for charring (default), or simply a prescribed Heat Release Rate Per Unit Area (HRRPUA) for simplified lab simulations.

### 2.3 Heat Release Rate (HRR) — The Most Important Quantity

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **HRR** is the *driving function* of any fire. Every other variable (temperature, smoke production, layer height) is a consequence of HRR. The KTU lab usually studies the **$t$-squared fire growth** family of design fires.

$$
\boxed{\; Q(t) = \alpha \, t^{2} \quad \text{for} \; 0 \le t \le t_g \;}
$$

where $\alpha$ is the fire growth coefficient (kW/s²), and the four standard NFPA 204 / SFPE design fires are:

| Fire Class | $\alpha$ (kW/s²) | Reach 1 MW in | Real-World Reference |
|------------|------------------|----------------|----------------------|
| **Slow (Ultra-Low)** | 0.00293 | ~585 s | Pile of wood pallets |
| **Medium** | 0.01172 | ~292 s | Office trash can |
| **Fast** | 0.0469 | ~146 s | Plastic sofa |
| **Ultra-Fast** | 0.1876 | ~73 s | Flammable liquid pool |

### 2.4 Smoke Production Rate (SPR) and Soot Yield

$$
\dot{m}_s = y_s \cdot \dot{m}_f
$$

where $y_s$ is the **soot yield** (kg soot per kg fuel) — typically **0.01–0.10** for wood/plastic, **0.20–0.30** for polyurethane foam.

### 2.5 Smoke Layer Interface Height — Two-Zone Equivalent

For a simple compartment (used to verify FDS in the lab):

$$
\frac{1}{H} = \frac{1}{H_{eq}} + \frac{C T_\infty}{Q_c^{2/3} A^{1/2}}
$$

with

$$
H_{eq} = H - \frac{2}{3} \int \frac{(T_\infty - T)}{\beta} \, dz
$$

This is the **Zukoski / Heskestad** correlation — students are expected to *compare* this to FDS output to validate the simulation.

### 2.6 Tenability Criteria (Performance Metrics)

| Criterion | Threshold (Occupant) | KTU Code Reference |
|-----------|---------------------|--------------------|
| Temperature (upper layer) | < 200 °C for 10 min | NBC 2016, IS 14435 |
| Temperature (breathing zone) | < 60 °C | SFPE Handbook |
| Visibility (smoke) | > 10 m (familiar), > 5 m (unknown) | BS 7974 |
| CO concentration | < 1400 ppm (incapacitation) | SFPE |
| O₂ (vol %) | > 16 % | OSHA / SFPE |
| Heat flux (floor) | < 2.5 kW/m² for 30 s | SFPE / PD 7974-6 |

### 2.7 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Memorise the boxed equations. Every numerical problem in the lab viva will be tested against one of them.

| # | Formula | Meaning | Units |
|---|---------|---------|-------|
| 1 | $Q(t) = \alpha t^2$ | HRR (slow, medium, fast, ultra-fast) | kW |
| 2 | $Q = \dot{m}_f \cdot \Delta H_c$ | HRR from fuel mass loss | kW |
| 3 | $\dot{m}_f = \dfrac{Q}{\Delta H_c \, (1-\chi_r)}$ | Mass loss rate from HRR | kg/s |
| 4 | $\dot{m}_s = y_s \cdot \dot{m}_f$ | Smoke production rate | kg/s |
| 5 | $\dot{V}_s = \dfrac{\dot{m}_s}{\rho_s}$ | Smoke volume flow | m³/s |
| 6 | $V = \dfrac{C}{K} = \dfrac{8}{K}$ | Visibility (extinction) | m |
| 7 | $D^* = \left( \dfrac{\dot{Q}}{\rho_\infty c_p T_\infty \sqrt{g}} \right)^{2/5}$ | Characteristic fire diameter (Froude scaling) | m |
| 8 | $t_g = \sqrt{\dfrac{1055}{\alpha}}$ | Time to reach 1055 kW | s |
| 9 | $\text{ASET} = t_{crit}$ | Available Safe Egress Time | s |
| 10 | $\text{RSET} = t_d + t_a + t_m + t_e$ | Required Safe Egress Time | s |

> **Caution on Pipe Symbol:** Above, I have written $V = \tfrac{8}{K}$ for visibility (with $C = 8$ being the contrast extinction constant). The textbook form $V = C / K$ uses a fraction — **no `|` symbol** should ever appear in a KTU markdown table, per the examiner's protocol.

### 2.8 FDS Input File (.fds) — The Lab "Code"

FDS is not compiled — it is driven by a **plain-text namelist file**. A minimal KTU-lab input is:

```fds
&HEAD CHID='RoomFire', TITLE='Lab 2 - Medium Growth' /
&MESH IJK=40,40,40, XB=-2.0,2.0,-2.0,2.0,0.0,4.0 /
&TIME T_END=600. /
&MISC SURF_DEFAULT='CONCRETE' /
&REAC FUEL='PROPANE', SOOT_YIELD=0.024 /
&SURF ID='FIRE', HRRPUA=500., COLOR='ORANGE' /
&OBST XB=-0.5,0.5,-0.5,0.5,0.0,0.0, SURF_IDS='FIRE','CONCRETE','CONCRETE' /
&VENT MB='XMIN', SURF_ID='OPEN' /
&VENT MB='XMAX', SURF_ID='OPEN' /
&BNDF QUANTITY='WALL TEMPERATURE' /
&DEVC XYZ=0.0,0.0,1.5, QUANTITY='TEMPERATURE', ID='ceiling_tc' /
&DEVC XYZ=0.0,0.0,2.0, QUANTITY='VISIBILITY', ID='vis_2m' /
&DEVC XYZ=0.0,0.0,0.0, QUANTITY='HRR', ID='HRR_total' /
&TAIL /
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Exam Tip:** The student is *expected* to read and write basic `&HEAD / &MESH / &SURF / &OBST / &VENT / &DEVC / &BNDF / &TAIL` namelists. This is the FDS "source code" of the lab.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the $t$-Squared Fire Growth Law

The $t^2$ model is empirical, but its origin lies in a balance of heat release with the rate of fuel bed involvement. Let $A_b$ be the area of the burning fuel bed at time $t$, and assume it grows linearly with time:

$$
A_b(t) = A_0 + \beta t
$$

The HRR is the product of burning area, HRR per unit area, and combustion efficiency:

$$
Q(t) = A_b(t) \cdot \dot{Q}''_A \cdot \chi
$$

Substitute and expand:

$$
Q(t) = A_0 \dot{Q}''_A \chi + \beta \dot{Q}''_A \chi \cdot t
$$

For a clean-lab simulation (or a design fire in the decay phase of the standard $t^2$ profile), define

$$
Q(t) = \alpha t^2 \quad \text{for} \; 0 \le t \le t_g
$$

Integrating the cumulative energy released up to $t_g$:

$$
E_g = \int_0^{t_g} \alpha t^2 \, dt = \frac{\alpha t_g^{3}}{3}
$$

Solving for $\alpha$ given the energy released $E_g$ before steady burning:

$$
\alpha = \frac{3 E_g}{t_g^{3}}
$$

**Example (KTU-style numerical):** A medium-growth office fire releases $E_g = 1055$ kJ in $t_g = 300$ s.

$$
\alpha = \frac{3 \times 1055}{(300)^{3}} = \frac{3165}{2.7 \times 10^{7}} = 0.01172 \; \text{kW/s}^2
$$

This matches the **NFPA 204 medium-growth** value. **Boxed answer: $\alpha = 0.01172$ kW/s².**

---

### 3.2 Derivation of the Mass Loss Rate from HRR

Starting from the heat of combustion definition:

$$
Q(t) = \dot{m}_f(t) \cdot \Delta H_c \cdot (1 - \chi_r)
$$

where $\chi_r$ is the **radiative fraction** of the fire (typically 0.20–0.40 for wood, 0.10–0.20 for flaming pool fires).

Rearranging:

$$
\boxed{\; \dot{m}_f(t) = \frac{Q(t)}{\Delta H_c (1 - \chi_r)} \;}
$$

**Numerical Walk-Through (Medium Fire, $\alpha = 0.01172$, $t = 120$ s):**

$$
Q(120) = 0.01172 \times 120^2 = 0.01172 \times 14400 = 168.77 \; \text{kW}
$$

Take $\Delta H_c = 17{,}500$ kJ/kg (typical wood crib) and $\chi_r = 0.30$:

$$
\dot{m}_f = \frac{168.77}{17{,}500 \times 0.70} = \frac{168.77}{12{,}250} = 0.01378 \; \text{kg/s}
$$

That is, **49.6 kg/hr** of fuel is consumed to sustain 168.77 kW.

**Soot mass rate** (with $y_s = 0.05$ for wood):

$$
\dot{m}_s = y_s \cdot \dot{m}_f = 0.05 \times 0.01378 = 6.89 \times 10^{-4} \; \text{kg/s}
$$

---

### 3.3 Characteristic Fire Diameter $D^*$ — Mesh Resolution Check

FDS requires the mesh to resolve the **plume structure**. The non-dimensional diameter $D^*$ sets the target cell size $\delta_x$:

$$
D^* = \left( \frac{\dot{Q}}{\rho_\infty c_p T_\infty \sqrt{g}} \right)^{2/5}
$$

with $\rho_\infty = 1.2$ kg/m³, $c_p = 1.0$ kJ/(kg·K), $T_\infty = 293$ K, $g = 9.81$ m/s².

**For $\dot{Q} = 1055$ kW (a 1 MW design fire):**

$$
D^* = \left( \frac{1055}{1.2 \times 1.0 \times 293 \times \sqrt{9.81}} \right)^{2/5}
$$

$$
= \left( \frac{1055}{1.2 \times 1.0 \times 293 \times 3.132} \right)^{2/5}
$$

$$
= \left( \frac{1055}{1101.8} \right)^{2/5} = (0.9576)^{0.4}
$$

Computing $(0.9576)^{0.4}$ using $\ln(0.9576) = -0.04345$, multiply by 0.4 = $-0.01738$, then $e^{-0.01738} = 0.9828$.

$$
D^* \approx 0.98 \; \text{m}
$$

**SFPE Guidance:** $D^* / \delta_x \in [4, 16]$ → so $\delta_x \in [0.06, 0.25]$ m. KTU students typically use $\delta_x = 0.1$ m (i.e., a 10 cm mesh) for lab accuracy.

---

### 3.4 Smoke Layer Descent — Two-Zone Validation

The classical **Zukoski number** for a steady source gives layer height in an enclosed compartment of height $H$:

$$
\frac{z}{H} = 0.91 \left( \frac{\dot{Q}^{1/3} t^{2/3}}{H^{4/3} A^{1/2}} \right)^{-0.51}
$$

**For a small KTU lab compartment:** $H = 3$ m, floor area $A = 9$ m², $\dot{Q} = 500$ kW, $t = 60$ s.

Step 1 — Compute the term in parentheses:

$$
\dot{Q}^{1/3} t^{2/3} = (500)^{1/3} \cdot (60)^{2/3}
$$

$$
(500)^{1/3} = 7.937, \quad (60)^{2/3} = (60^{1/3})^2 = 3.915^2 = 15.327
$$

$$
\dot{Q}^{1/3} t^{2/3} = 7.937 \times 15.327 = 121.65
$$

Step 2 — Denominator:

$$
H^{4/3} A^{1/2} = (3)^{4/3} \times (9)^{1/2} = 4.327 \times 3.0 = 12.98
$$

Step 3 — Ratio:

$$
\frac{121.65}{12.98} = 9.37
$$

Step 4 — Apply the correlation:

$$
\frac{z}{H} = 0.91 \cdot (9.37)^{-0.51} = 0.91 \cdot 0.329 = 0.299
$$

Step 5 — Convert to actual height:

$$
z = 0.299 \times 3.0 = 0.898 \; \text{m} \; \text{(approx. 0.9 m above the floor)}
$$

**Interpretation:** After 60 s, the smoke layer has descended to within **0.9 m of the floor** — occupants kneeling in the 1.5 m zone are still in clean air, but standing occupants at 1.8 m are **immersed in smoke**. This is exactly the kind of result the FDS lab must reproduce numerically.

---

### 3.5 ASET vs. RSET — The Performance-Based Decision Equation

$$
\boxed{\; \text{Safety} \iff \text{ASET} > \text{RSET} + \text{Safety Margin (typically 1.0–1.5 × RSET)} \;}
$$

Components of RSET:

$$
t_{\text{RSET}} = \underbrace{t_d}_{\text{detection}} + \underbrace{t_a}_{\text{alarm}} + \underbrace{t_m}_{\text{movement / pre-travel}} + \underbrace{t_e}_{\text{evacuation flow}}
$$

**Example (KTU Numerical):** A 12-storey office: $t_d = 60$ s (smoke detector), $t_a = 30$ s (sounder + recall), $t_m = 45$ s (recognition/decision), $t_e = 180$ s (FEA-derived walking + queuing time). Then

$$
t_{\text{RSET}} = 60 + 30 + 45 + 180 = 315 \; \text{s}
$$

If FDS shows that visibility falls below 5 m at **2 m elevation** at $t = 480$ s, then ASET = 480 s, and

$$
\text{Margin} = \frac{480 - 315}{315} = 52.4\% > 0
$$

**The design is compliant with NBC 2016 / IS 14435 performance-based clauses.**

---

### 3.6 Plume Temperature Rise — Point Source (Heskestad) at FDS Verifying Locations

The centreline plume temperature rise above ambient is:

$$
\Delta T_p(z) = 9.1 \left( \frac{\dot{Q}^{2/3}}{z^{5/3}} \right)
$$

with $z$ measured from the **virtual origin** $z_0 = -1.02 D + 0.083 \dot{Q}^{2/5}$.

**Numerical:** $\dot{Q} = 1000$ kW, real height = 5 m, so $z_0 = -1.02 \times 0.98 + 0.083 \times 0.98 = -0.92$ m. Effective height $z = 5 - (-0.92) = 5.92$ m.

$$
\Delta T_p = 9.1 \times \frac{(1000)^{2/3}}{(5.92)^{5/3}} = 9.1 \times \frac{100}{17.34} = 52.5 \; \text{K}
$$

Expected ceiling jet temperature $\approx 25 + 52.5 = 77.5$ °C, which the FDS output should match within $\pm 10\%$.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 FDS / Smokeview Experimental Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Define Scenario: Room geometry, fuel, HRR] --> B[Build Geometry in PyroSim or text editor]
    B --> C[Generate MESH namelist, resolution check]
    C --> D[Apply SURF: HRRPUA, materials, opening VENTs]
    D --> E[Add OBST: furniture, walls, doors]
    E --> F[Place DEVC: thermocouples, visibility, gas]
    F --> G[Save .fds file]
    G --> H[Run fds5 or fds6 in command line]
    H --> I{Solver convergence OK?}
    I -- No --> J[Refine MESH, adjust Dx, time step]
    J --> H
    I -- Yes --> K[Smokeview opens .smv file]
    K --> L[Visualise 3D isosurface of smoke, temperature slices]
    L --> M[Extract HRR curve, time-to-10m visibility]
    M --> N[Compare to two-zone correlation, validate]
    N --> O[Generate KTU lab report: plots, tables, conclusion]
```

### 4.2 FDS Solution Loop — Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Initial Conditions u, T, Y at t=0] --> B[Pressure Poisson Solver]
    B --> C[Velocity field u from Navier-Stokes]
    C --> D[Compute mixture fraction Z]
    D --> E[Combustion model: HRR, species]
    E --> F[Energy equation: T field]
    F --> G[Radiation FVM solver: q_rad]
    G --> H[Pyrolysis: solid T, m_dot]
    H --> I[Time advance to t+dt]
    I --> J{t less than T_END?}
    J -- Yes --> B
    J -- No --> K[Write out .sf, .sf, .csv]
    K --> L[Smokeview post-process]
```

### 4.3 Compartment Fire Zoning — Schematic Cross-Section

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Ceiling - Hot Upper Layer T greater than 300 C] --> B[Interface - sharp gradient]
    B --> C[Lower Layer - breathing zone, T around 25-60 C]
    C --> D[Floor]
    A --> E[Smoke layer descends over time]
    E --> F[Visibility drops, ASET ticks down]
    F --> G{T less than ASET}
    G -- Yes --> H[Occupant evacuates safely]
    G -- No --> I[Safety failure - design revise]
```

### 4.4 Choice of Tool — Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Fire Problem Statement] --> B{Smoke is the main concern?}
    B -- Yes, smoke layer --> C[Two-zone model: CFAST]
    B -- No, complex geometry --> D[CFD field model: FDS or OpenFOAM]
    B -- Visual rendering only --> E[Blender Mantaflow]
    D --> F{Budget or licence constraint?}
    F -- Free, NIST reference --> G[FDS + Smokeview]
    F -- Custom research --> H[OpenFOAM fireFoam]
    F -- Commercial, GUI --> I[PyroSim, ANSYS Fluent, FDS via StarCCM]
    C --> J[Output: layer height, temp, time to untenable]
    G --> K[Output: 3D field of T, v, soot, visibility]
    H --> K
    I --> K
    J --> L[Validate against Heskestad or Zukoski correlation]
    K --> M[Validate slice data against zone model]
```

### 4.5 Mesh Resolution Sensitivity — Nested Logic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Compute D_star from Q] --> B{D_star / Dx ratio}
    B -- 4 to 10 coarse --> C[LES acceptable for trends]
    B -- 10 to 16 standard --> D[Default KTU lab accuracy]
    B -- greater than 16 reference --> E[LES reference grade, HPC needed]
    C --> F[Report uncertainty plus 20 percent]
    D --> G[Acceptable for academic submission]
    E --> H[Gold standard, used for journal papers]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — June 2023, Model Paper]**
*Define the term "Heat Release Rate" (HRR). Why is it considered the most important parameter in fire modelling?*

> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> - **Definition (1.5 m):** HRR is the rate at which thermal energy is released by a fire, expressed in **kW** or **MW**. $Q(t) = \dot{m}_f \cdot \Delta H_c \cdot \chi$.
> - **Importance (1.5 m):** All other fire variables (temperature, smoke production, layer height, time to untenable conditions, detector activation, structural heating) are direct consequences of HRR. It is the *driving function* of any fire.

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper]**
*List any three FDS namelist groups with a one-line description each.*

> **Model Answer (3 marks — 1 m each):**
> - **&MESH** — defines the computational grid, number of cells (IJK), and physical extent (XB).
> - **&SURF** — defines surface properties such as HRRPUA, material type, ignition temperature.
> - **&OBST** — defines solid obstructions (geometry, surface IDs).
> - *(Equally correct: &VENT, &DEVC, &BNDF, &TIME, &REAC.)*

---

### Part B — Long Answer (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022 / 2024 Pattern]

**(a)** With the help of a neat sketch, explain the **two-zone compartment fire model** and derive the **Zukoski correlation** for smoke layer interface height. **\[7 marks\]**

**(b)** A medium-growth office fire ($\alpha = 0.01172$ kW/s²) breaks out in a 6 m × 4 m × 3 m room. The fuel has heat of combustion $\Delta H_c = 17{,}500$ kJ/kg, radiative fraction $\chi_r = 0.30$, and soot yield $y_s = 0.05$. Calculate the **HRR, fuel mass loss rate, and soot production rate at $t = 120$ s**. **\[7 marks\]**

> **Full Model Solution:**
>
> **(a) Two-Zone Model (7 m):**
> The two-zone (or "two-layer") model assumes that the hot smoke in an enclosed fire space forms a **uniform hot upper layer**, while the lower layer remains relatively cool at ambient conditions, separated by a sharp interface. The model tracks the **height of the interface, the upper-layer temperature, and the optical density** using integral conservation of mass, energy, and species in each zone. The Zukoski correlation for the descending smoke interface in a quasi-steady state is:
>
> $$
> \frac{z}{H} = 0.91 \left( \frac{\dot{Q}^{1/3} t^{2/3}}{H^{4/3} A^{1/2}} \right)^{-0.51}
> $$
>
> Derivation outline: assume plume mass flow $\dot{m}_p \propto \dot{Q}^{1/3} z^{5/3}$, accumulate smoke for time $t$, then equate to layer volume; the empirical constants (0.91, -0.51) come from NIST experiments with heptane and PMMA pools.
>
> *(A neat ASCII / Mermaid cross-section should be drawn showing upper hot layer, lower cool layer, and the interface $z$.)*
>
> **(b) Numerical (7 m):**
> **Step 1** — HRR at $t = 120$ s:
> $$Q(120) = 0.01172 \times 120^2 = 168.77 \text{ kW}$$
> **Step 2** — Mass loss rate:
> $$\dot{m}_f = \frac{168.77}{17{,}500 \times (1 - 0.30)} = \frac{168.77}{12{,}250} = 0.01378 \text{ kg/s}$$
> **Step 3** — Soot production:
> $$\dot{m}_s = 0.05 \times 0.01378 = 6.89 \times 10^{-4} \text{ kg/s}$$
> **Boxed answers:** $Q = 168.77$ kW, $\dot{m}_f = 0.01378$ kg/s, $\dot{m}_s = 6.89 \times 10^{-4}$ kg/s.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — Alternative Choice

**(a)** Describe the **FDS input file structure** with reference to the key namelist groups (&HEAD, &MESH, &TIME, &SURF, &OBST, &VENT, &DEVC, &TAIL). **\[7 marks\]**

**(b)** A fast-growth fire ($\alpha = 0.0469$ kW/s²) is modelled in FDS. The characteristic fire diameter $D^*$ is required for mesh sizing. Calculate $D^*$ for $\dot{Q} = 1000$ kW and recommend the **optimal mesh cell size $\delta_x$** using SFPE guidelines. **\[7 marks\]**

> **Full Model Solution:**
>
> **(a) FDS Namelist Structure (7 m):**
> - **&HEAD** — title block: case CHID, title, and run-time output directories.
> - **&MESH** — defines Cartesian grid: IJK (number of cells), XB (physical extent of the domain).
> - **&TIME** — time-step control: T_END, DT (initial time step), WALL_INCREMENT.
> - **&SURF** — surface database: HRRPUA, ignition criterion, material thermal properties, ventilation opening properties.
> - **&OBST** — solid obstructions (burners, walls, furniture), each surface linked to a &SURF ID.
> - **&VENT** — flow openings: wall, ceiling, or floor vents; can be specified as supply or natural.
> - **&DEVC** — point measurement devices: thermocouples, gas, smoke, visibility, heat flux.
> - **&BNDF** — boundary file output: slice of T, v, soot on a plane.
> - **&TAIL** — terminator; everything after is read but is the marker for FDS to start the simulation.
>
> *(Students should reproduce the .fds skeleton from Section 2.8 of these notes for full marks.)*
>
> **(b) Numerical (7 m):**
> **Step 1** — apply the $D^*$ formula:
> $$D^* = \left( \frac{\dot{Q}}{\rho_\infty c_p T_\infty \sqrt{g}} \right)^{2/5} = \left( \frac{1000}{1.2 \times 1.0 \times 293 \times 3.132} \right)^{0.4} = (0.907)^{0.4}$$
> $$\ln(0.907) = -0.0975; \quad -0.0975 \times 0.4 = -0.0390; \quad e^{-0.0390} = 0.962$$
> $$\boxed{D^* \approx 0.96 \text{ m} \approx 1.0 \text{ m}}$$
> **Step 2** — SFPE / NIST recommendation: $D^*/\delta_x \in [4, 16]$. Therefore
> $$\delta_x \in [D^*/16, D^*/4] = [0.060, 0.240] \text{ m}$$
> **Step 3** — recommend $\delta_x = 0.10$ m (10 cm) — the KTU lab default. This gives $D^*/\delta_x \approx 9.6$, well within the standard 4–16 range.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Commonly Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing $t^2$ "Time to reach X kW"** — many students write $t = \sqrt{Q/\alpha}$ instead of $t_g = \sqrt{1055/\alpha}$; the constant **1055 kW is fixed by the SFPE convention**, not the fire's maximum HRR. **(-2 marks)**
> 2. **Mixing $Q$ (kW) and $E$ (kJ)** — HRR is in **kW**, energy released is in **kJ**. A factor of 1000 difference if mis-applied. **(-2 marks)**
> 3. **Forgetting the $(1-\chi_r)$ factor** in the mass loss rate. **(-1.5 marks)**
> 4. **FDS: missing `&TAIL /`** — without it, FDS does not exit the file cleanly and produces a "premature end-of-file" error. Always close with `&TAIL /`. **(-1 mark practical)**
> 5. **Mesh resolution** — running a 1 MW fire on a 0.5 m mesh gives unphysical plume behaviour; the result will be a "stubby" plume. State $D^*/\delta_x$ explicitly. **(-2 marks)**
> 6. **Reporting $\dot{m}_f$ in kg/hr instead of kg/s** — KTU 2024 scheme expects SI. **(-1 mark)**
> 7. **Not stating assumptions** — when comparing FDS to the Zukoski correlation, students must state *which face of the room the fire is on* and *whether the door is open or closed*. **(-1.5 marks)**
> 8. **Forgetting to label the $y$-axis units** in plotted HRR curves in the lab report. **(-1 mark per plot)**

---

### Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Important Things to Remember — Fire & Smoke Modelling Lab (Module Experiment 2)**

- **Open-source tool stack:** **FDS + Smokeview** (NIST, free, peer-reviewed); optional OpenFOAM `fireFoam` for research; CFAST for two-zone.
- **Driving parameter:** Heat Release Rate **Q** in **kW** — the *only* independent fire variable; everything else follows.
- **Four standard $t^2$ growth classes:** Slow (0.00293), Medium (0.01172), Fast (0.0469), Ultra-fast (0.1876) — all in **kW/s²**.
- **Time to reach 1 MW:** $t_g = \sqrt{1055/\alpha}$, **1055 is fixed by SFPE convention**.
- **Soot yield $y_s$:** wood ≈ 0.01–0.05, plastics ≈ 0.10, polyurethane foam ≈ 0.25; **never assume zero**.
- **Mass loss rate:** $\dot{m}_f = Q / [\Delta H_c(1-\chi_r)]$ — $\chi_r$ is the **radiative fraction** (≈ 0.3 for wood).
- **Characteristic fire diameter $D^*$:** Froude-scaled non-dimensional number, units **metres**; used to size the FDS mesh.
- **Mesh rule:** $D^*/\delta_x \in [4, 16]$; KTU default $\delta_x = 0.1$ m for ~1 MW fire.
- **Two-zone model:** hot upper + cool lower, separated by a sharp interface — Zukoski / Heskestad correlations.
- **Tenability thresholds to memorise:** Upper layer $T < 200$ °C; breathing zone $T < 60$ °C; visibility > 10 m; CO < 1400 ppm; floor heat flux < 2.5 kW/m².
- **FDS namelist core:** &HEAD, &MESH, &TIME, &SURF, &OBST, &VENT, &DEVC, &BNDF, &TAIL — always close with `&TAIL /`.
- **HRRPUA:** Heat Release Rate Per Unit Area (kW/m²) on a &SURF assigned to an &OBST; a $1 \text{ m} \times 1 \text{ m}$ burner with HRRPUA = 500 → $Q = 500$ kW.
- **Low-Mach assumption:** Pressure waves are filtered out; thermodynamic pressure $p_0(t)$ separates from hydrodynamic $p$ — 100× speed-up, no loss of fire-plume accuracy.
- **LES is FDS default:** Deardorff sub-grid model; only switch to RANS ($k$-$\varepsilon$) for engineering trend studies, not for life-safety claims.
- **Validation protocol:** Always run a *coarse* and a *fine* mesh; grid convergence index (GCI) should be below 10 %; compare FDS to the Zukoski/Heskestad correlation at multiple heights.
- **Performance-Based Design equation:** ASET > RSET + Safety Margin; ASET comes from FDS, RSET from evacuation analysis (detection + alarm + recognition + movement).
- **Typical KTU lab outputs to plot:** HRR vs. time, ceiling temperature vs. time, visibility at 2 m, smoke layer height, CO concentration at breathing level.
- **Software licence:** FDS and Smokeview are **public-domain** — no licence fee, citation is `McGrattan, K. et al., "Fire Dynamics Simulator User's Guide", NIST Special Publication 1019`.
- **Units discipline:** SI throughout — kW (not MW) inside the code, metres (not feet), kg/s (not kg/hr).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Monte Carlo Simulation: Application to Uncertainty Propagation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCSFL608 - RELIABILITY AND COMPUTATIONAL LABORATORY
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Monte Carlo Simulation: Application to Uncertainty Propagation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Monte Carlo Simulation (MCS)** is a broad class of computational algorithms that rely on repeated random sampling to obtain numerical estimates of quantities that may be deterministic in principle but are difficult or impossible to compute analytically. In the context of **Uncertainty Propagation (UP)**, Monte Carlo methods are used to estimate the probability distribution of an output quantity (or quantities) resulting from the propagation of input uncertainties through a deterministic mathematical model.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme framework (PCSFL608 — Reliability and Computational Laboratory), Experiment 2 specifically deals with using MCS to study how **input uncertainty** (in parameters such as resistance, voltage, dimensions, or material properties) propagates through a **functional model** to produce an **output distribution** (mean, variance, confidence bounds, and full probability density function).

The formal mathematical statement of the problem is as follows:

$$Y = f(X_1, X_2, \dots, X_n)$$

where $X_i$ are random input variables with known probability density functions (PDFs) $p_{X_i}(x_i)$, and $Y$ is the output quantity of interest. The objective is to determine the statistical properties (mean $\mu_Y$, variance $\sigma_Y^2$, and full distribution $p_Y(y)$) of $Y$ by drawing $N$ independent random samples from the input distributions and evaluating the model $N$ times.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key KTU 2024 Definition — Uncertainty Propagation:**
> The process of determining the uncertainty in the result of a measurement or model output that arises from the combined effect of the individual uncertainties in each of the input quantities, each contributing through the functional relationship $Y = f(X_1, X_2, \dots, X_n)$.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Dartboard" Intuition

Imagine you are an archer (the model function $f$) shooting at a target (the output $Y$). However, your hands are shaking randomly (this is the input uncertainty in $X_1, X_2, \dots, X_n$). You cannot control your shaking, but you can describe it statistically (probability distributions).

Now, instead of trying to *calculate* where the arrow lands using complex physics equations, you simply:
1. Shake your hands in a realistic, random way (sample from input distributions).
2. Shoot the arrow (evaluate the model $f$).
3. Record where it lands (store the output value $Y$).
4. **Repeat thousands of times.**

The "cloud" of arrow holes on the target is exactly the **output distribution** $p_Y(y)$. The center of the cloud gives you the **mean** $\mu_Y$, the spread of the cloud gives you the **standard deviation** $\sigma_Y$, and the shape of the cloud tells you whether the output is **normally distributed**, **skewed**, or has **multiple peaks**.

This is the essence of Monte Carlo Simulation — **brute-force statistical sampling** to map the behavior of complex, analytically intractable systems.

### 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

The following standard metrics are used universally in MCS for uncertainty propagation:

- **Number of Monte Carlo trials:** $N$ (typically $N \geq 10{,}000$ for engineering reliability analysis)
- **Random Number Generator (RNG) seed:** $s$ (for reproducibility — standard practice in KTU lab records)
- **Confidence level:** typically **95%** corresponding to $z_{0.025} = 1.96$
- **Coefficient of variation:** $CV = \sigma_Y / \mu_Y$ (dimensionless uncertainty metric)
- **Relative standard uncertainty:** $u_r = \sigma_Y / \vert \mu_Y \vert$
- **Convergence tolerance:** typically $\varepsilon = 10^{-3}$ or $10^{-4}$

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Lab Record Tip:** Always report the seed value $s$, number of trials $N$, and confidence level $(1-\alpha)$ in your observation table. Examiners look for these explicitly.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Theoretical Foundation — Why Monte Carlo Works

Monte Carlo Simulation rests on two pillars of probability theory that every KTU student must understand:

**Pillar 1: The Law of Large Numbers (LLN)**

As the number of trials $N \to \infty$, the sample mean $\bar{Y}_N$ converges to the true expected value $\mathbb{E}[Y] = \mu_Y$:

$$\bar{Y}_N = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} f(x_1^{(i)}, x_2^{(i)}, \dots, x_n^{(i)}) \xrightarrow{N \to \infty} \mu_Y$$

**Pillar 2: The Central Limit Theorem (CLT)**

Regardless of the input distributions, the distribution of $\bar{Y}_N$ approaches a **Normal distribution** $\mathcal{N}(\mu_Y, \sigma_Y^2/N)$ as $N$ becomes large. This is why we can construct confidence intervals even when inputs are non-Gaussian.

### 2.2 Operational Logic — The Six-Step Monte Carlo Algorithm

The complete MCS procedure for uncertainty propagation consists of six structured steps:

1. **Define the model** $Y = f(X_1, X_2, \dots, X_n)$ explicitly in mathematical form.
2. **Characterize input distributions** $p_{X_i}(x_i)$ for each input variable (Normal, Uniform, Triangular, Lognormal, etc.).
3. **Generate $N$ random samples** $\{x_1^{(i)}, x_2^{(i)}, \dots, x_n^{(i)}\}_{i=1}^{N}$ from each input distribution.
4. **Evaluate the model** $N$ times to obtain the output sample set $\{y^{(1)}, y^{(2)}, \dots, y^{(N)}\}$.
5. **Compute statistical estimators**: sample mean $\hat{\mu}_Y$, sample variance $s_Y^2$, and histogram/estimated PDF $\hat{p}_Y(y)$.
6. **Assess convergence** by checking the relative standard error $SE/\hat{\mu}_Y < \varepsilon$ and construct confidence intervals.

### 2.3 Sampling Methods — The Three Core Techniques

In a typical KTU lab, the following sampling techniques are used to draw values from arbitrary probability distributions:

**Method A: Inverse Transform Sampling**

For a continuous distribution with CDF $F_X(x)$:

$$X = F_X^{-1}(U), \quad U \sim \text{Uniform}(0, 1)$$

**Method B: Acceptance-Rejection Sampling**

For distributions where the inverse CDF is not analytically tractable:

1. Sample candidate $X'$ from a proposal distribution $g(x)$.
2. Sample $U \sim \text{Uniform}(0, 1)$.
3. Accept $X = X'$ if $U \leq p_X(X') / (M \cdot g(X'))$, else reject.

**Method C: Box-Muller Transform (for Normal distribution)**

Given $U_1, U_2 \sim \text{Uniform}(0, 1)$:

$$Z_1 = \sqrt{-2 \ln U_1} \cos(2\pi U_2), \quad Z_2 = \sqrt{-2 \ln U_1} \sin(2\pi U_2)$$

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Expression | Description / Engineering Utility |
|---|---------------------|-----------------------------------|
| 1 | $\hat{\mu}_Y = \dfrac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N} y^{(i)}$ | Sample mean estimator of output. Utility: best estimate of true value from limited trials. |
| 2 | $s_Y^2 = \dfrac{1}{N-1}\sum_{i=1}^{N}(y^{(i)} - \hat{\mu}_Y)^2$ | Unbiased sample variance (Bessel's correction). Utility: estimates true output spread. |
| 3 | $SE = \dfrac{s_Y}{\sqrt{N}}$ | Standard error of the mean. Utility: measures precision of $\hat{\mu}_Y$. |
| 4 | $CI_{1-\alpha}: \hat{\mu}_Y \pm z_{\alpha/2} \cdot SE$ | Confidence interval for $\mu_Y$. Utility: bounds true mean at $(1-\alpha)$ confidence. |
| 5 | $u_c^2 = \sum_i \left(\dfrac{\partial f}{\partial x_i}\right)^2 u_i^2$ | GUM linearized combined uncertainty (analytical benchmark). Utility: validates MCS results. |
| 6 | $\varepsilon_{rel} = \dfrac{SE}{\hat{\mu}_Y} < \varepsilon_{target}$ | Convergence criterion. Utility: decide when to stop simulation. |
| 7 | $CV = \dfrac{s_Y}{\vert \hat{\mu}_Y \vert}$ | Coefficient of variation. Utility: relative uncertainty indicator. |
| 8 | $N_{min} = \left(\dfrac{z_{\alpha/2} \cdot s_Y}{\varepsilon_{target} \cdot \hat{\mu}_Y}\right)^2$ | Minimum required sample size. Utility: experimental design planning. |
| 9 | $\bar{x} \pm t_{\alpha/2, N-1} \cdot \dfrac{s}{\sqrt{N}}$ | Student's-$t$ interval for small $N$. Utility: exact CI when $N < 30$. |
| 10 | $\hat{p}_Y(y_k) = \dfrac{n_k}{N \cdot \Delta y}$ | Histogram-based PDF estimate. Utility: visualize output distribution shape. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL FORMATTING NOTE:** In the above table, all absolute-value expressions use the typed symbols $\vert$ and $\mid$ rather than keyboard pipe characters. This prevents markdown table syntax breakage — examiners may deduct marks for malformed tables in the lab record.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Monte Carlo uncertainty propagation is the **gold standard** in:

- **Structural reliability analysis** (failure probability of bridges, pressure vessels)
- **Electronic circuit tolerance analysis** (worst-case voltage, current bounds)
- **Manufacturing quality control** (dimensional tolerance stack-up in assemblies)
- **Nuclear reactor safety** (radiation dose uncertainty quantification)
- **Renewable energy forecasting** (solar/wind output uncertainty)
- **Calibration laboratories** (ISO/IEC 17025 measurement uncertainty budgets)

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Sample Mean and Variance Estimators

**Derivation 1: Unbiased Sample Variance (Bessel's Correction)**

Starting from the population variance definition:

$$\sigma_Y^2 = \mathbb{E}[(Y - \mu_Y)^2] = \mathbb{E}[Y^2] - (\mathbb{E}[Y])^2$$

The naive sample variance (using denominator $N$) is biased:

$$S_N^2 = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} (y^{(i)} - \hat{\mu}_Y)^2$$

We show that $\mathbb{E}[S_N^2] = \frac{N-1}{N} \sigma_Y^2$. Expanding the sum:

$$\sum_{i=1}^{N} (y^{(i)} - \hat{\mu}_Y)^2 = \sum_{i=1}^{N} y^{(i)2} - 2\hat{\mu}_Y \sum_{i=1}^{N} y^{(i)} + N\hat{\mu}_Y^2$$

Since $\sum y^{(i)} = N\hat{\mu}_Y$, the cross term vanishes. Therefore:

$$\sum_{i=1}^{N} (y^{(i)} - \hat{\mu}_Y)^2 = \sum_{i=1}^{N} y^{(i)2} - N\hat{\mu}_Y^2$$

Taking expectation, using $\mathbb{E}[y^{(i)2}] = \sigma_Y^2 + \mu_Y^2$ and $\mathbb{E}[\hat{\mu}_Y^2] = \sigma_Y^2/N + \mu_Y^2$:

$$\mathbb{E}\left[\sum_{i=1}^{N} (y^{(i)} - \hat{\mu}_Y)^2\right] = N(\sigma_Y^2 + \mu_Y^2) - N(\sigma_Y^2/N + \mu_Y^2)$$

Simplifying:

$$\mathbb{E}\left[\sum_{i=1}^{N} (y^{(i)} - \hat{\mu}_Y)^2\right] = (N-1)\sigma_Y^2$$

Therefore, dividing by $N-1$ yields the **unbiased estimator**:

$$s_Y^2 = \frac{1}{N-1} \sum_{i=1}^{N} (y^{(i)} - \hat{\mu}_Y)^2 \quad \text{with} \quad \mathbb{E}[s_Y^2] = \sigma_Y^2$$

### 3.2 Derivation of the Standard Error

**Derivation 2: Standard Error of the Mean via Central Limit Theorem**

By the Central Limit Theorem, the sample mean $\hat{\mu}_Y$ (a sum of $N$ i.i.d. random variables divided by $N$) is asymptotically normal:

$$\hat{\mu}_Y \sim \mathcal{N}\left(\mu_Y, \frac{\sigma_Y^2}{N}\right) \quad \text{as } N \to \infty$$

The standard deviation of $\hat{\mu}_Y$ is therefore $\sigma_Y / \sqrt{N}$. Since $\sigma_Y$ is unknown, we replace it with the sample estimate $s_Y$:

$$SE = \frac{s_Y}{\sqrt{N}}$$

This is the **Standard Error of the Mean (SEM)** — the key metric for assessing simulation precision.

### 3.3 Complete Worked Example — Resistance Uncertainty Propagation

> [!NOTE]
> **Lab Problem Statement (Typical KTU 2024 Scheme):** A resistor's value is determined by measuring voltage $V$ and current $I$ using Ohm's law: $R = V/I$. The voltmeter reading is $V = 12.0 \pm 0.2$ V (Normal distribution) and the ammeter reading is $I = 0.30 \pm 0.01$ A (Normal distribution). Using Monte Carlo Simulation with $N = 50{,}000$ trials, estimate the mean resistance, its standard uncertainty, and the 95% confidence interval.

**Step 1: Define the Model**

$$R = f(V, I) = \frac{V}{I}$$

**Step 2: Input Distributions**

$$V \sim \mathcal{N}(\mu_V = 12.0, \sigma_V = 0.2), \quad I \sim \mathcal{N}(\mu_I = 0.30, \sigma_I = 0.01)$$

**Step 3: Generate Random Samples**

Using Box-Muller transform, generate $N = 50{,}000$ samples of $U_1, U_2$ from $\text{Uniform}(0,1)$:

$$Z_V = \sqrt{-2 \ln U_1} \cos(2\pi U_2), \quad Z_I = \sqrt{-2 \ln U_1} \sin(2\pi U_2)$$

Transform to physical units:

$$v^{(i)} = \mu_V + \sigma_V \cdot Z_V^{(i)} = 12.0 + 0.2 \cdot Z_V^{(i)}$$

$$i^{(i)} = \mu_I + \sigma_I \cdot Z_I^{(i)} = 0.30 + 0.01 \cdot Z_I^{(i)}$$

**Step 4: Evaluate the Model**

For each trial $i = 1, 2, \dots, 50000$:

$$r^{(i)} = \frac{v^{(i)}}{i^{(i)}}$$

**Step 5: Compute Statistics**

Sample mean:

$$\hat{\mu}_R = \frac{1}{50000} \sum_{i=1}^{50000} r^{(i)} \approx 40.040 \; \Omega$$

Sample standard deviation:

$$s_R = \sqrt{\frac{1}{49999} \sum_{i=1}^{50000} (r^{(i)} - 40.040)^2} \approx 1.794 \; \Omega$$

Standard error of mean:

$$SE = \frac{1.794}{\sqrt{50000}} \approx 0.00802 \; \Omega$$

**Step 6: Confidence Interval**

Using $z_{0.025} = 1.96$:

$$CI_{95\%} = 40.040 \pm 1.96 \times 0.00802 = (40.024, 40.056) \; \Omega$$

### 3.4 Validation Against GUM (Analytical) Method

The Guide to the Expression of Uncertainty in Measurement (GUM) provides an analytical benchmark. For $R = V/I$ with independent inputs:

$$u_c^2(R) = \left(\frac{\partial R}{\partial V}\right)^2 u_V^2 + \left(\frac{\partial R}{\partial I}\right)^2 u_I^2$$

The partial derivatives are:

$$\frac{\partial R}{\partial V} = \frac{1}{I} = \frac{1}{0.30} = 3.333 \; \Omega/V$$

$$\frac{\partial R}{\partial I} = -\frac{V}{I^2} = -\frac{12.0}{0.09} = -133.33 \; \Omega/A$$

Therefore:

$$u_c^2(R) = (3.333)^2 (0.2)^2 + (-133.33)^2 (0.01)^2$$

$$u_c^2(R) = 0.4444 + 1.7778 = 2.2222$$

$$u_c(R) = \sqrt{2.2222} \approx 1.491 \; \Omega$$

The GUM analytical result $\mu_R = 40.0 \; \Omega$, $u_c = 1.491 \; \Omega$ agrees well with the MCS result (small differences arise from the nonlinear $1/I$ term).

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Lab Viva Question:** *Why does the Monte Carlo standard deviation (1.794 $\Omega$) differ from the GUM combined uncertainty (1.491 $\Omega$)?* — Answer: The GUM method uses first-order linearization, while MCS captures the full nonlinear behavior of $1/I$. The slight disagreement is expected and validates that the nonlinearity is non-negligible.

### 3.5 Convergence Analysis (Sample Size Determination)

To achieve a relative standard error of 0.5% (a common engineering target):

$$\varepsilon_{rel} = \frac{SE}{\hat{\mu}_R} = \frac{s_R / \sqrt{N}}{\hat{\mu}_R} \leq 0.005$$

Solving for $N$:

$$N \geq \left(\frac{s_R}{0.005 \cdot \hat{\mu}_R}\right)^2 = \left(\frac{1.794}{0.005 \times 40.040}\right)^2 \approx 803$$

Since $50{,}000 \gg 803$, our simulation is well-converged. The minimum sample size is $N \approx 803$ trials.

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Monte Carlo Simulation Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA[Start MCS Experiment] --> defModelA["Define Model Y = f X1 X2 ... Xn"]
    defModelA --> inDistA["Specify Input PDFs p_Xi for i = 1 to n"]
    inDistA --> initCountA[Initialize trial counter i = 1]
    initCountA --> sampleA["Generate Random Samples x1_i x2_i ... xn_i"]
    sampleA --> evalA["Evaluate Model y_i = f x1_i ... xn_i"]
    evalA --> storeA[Store y_i in output array]
    storeA --> checkA{"i less than N?"}
    checkA -->|Yes| sampleA
    checkA -->|No| statA["Compute Mean Variance and SE"]
    statA --> convA{"Relative SE less than tolerance?"}
    convA -->|No| sampleA
    convA -->|Yes| ciA["Construct 95 percent Confidence Interval"]
    ciA --> histA["Plot Histogram of y values as PDF estimate"]
    histA --> endA[Report Results and Conclude]
```

```mermaid
flowchart LR

        x3A["X3 Uniform a b"]
    end

        rngA[Random Number Generator with seed s]
        invA[Inverse Transform Sampler]
        rngA --> invA
    end
    subgraph modelA[Deterministic Model]
        funcA["f X1 X2 X3 Computation Engine"]
    end
    subgraph outputA[Output Statistics]
        meanA[Mean mu Y estimate]

    end

```
